categories.title: Under the Hood
categories.description: Prompt assembly, tool selection, and context management

workflows.title: Launching Claude Code
workflows.subtitle: From terminal command to interactive REPL
workflows.userAction: Runs `claude` in terminal
workflows.userSees: Animated splash, then a blinking cursor in an interactive REPL

detail.whatUserSees: Terminal accepts the command and a brief pause follows
detail.whatCodeDoes: main.tsx is the entry point. Commander.js parses CLI flags (--model, --resume, --print, --allowedTools, etc.) and determines which entrypoint to invoke.
detail.insight: Commander.js is configured with 40+ flags, but most users never pass any — the defaults are chosen to make bare `claude` the happy path.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible yet — sub-second initialization
detail.whatCodeDoes: init() in entrypoints/init.ts runs config validation, sets up graceful shutdown handlers (SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGHUP), initializes telemetry, and configures the process environment.
detail.insight: The graceful shutdown handler is registered before anything else — even if init itself crashes, the terminal state will be cleaned up properly.

detail.whatUserSees: Still no visible change — prefetch happens in background
detail.whatCodeDoes: startMdmRawRead() fetches managed device management settings and startKeychainPrefetch() reads cached credentials concurrently. Both are fire-and-forget promises that resolve before they are needed.
detail.insight: MDM settings are fetched even for personal users — the code path is identical for enterprise and individual, just the MDM response is empty for non-managed devices.

detail.whatUserSees: If auth fails, a login prompt appears instead of the REPL
detail.whatCodeDoes: Checks credentials in priority order: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var (highest), then keychain OAuth token, then no credentials. The first valid credential wins.
detail.insight: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY always takes precedence over OAuth — this lets CI pipelines and scripts override interactive credentials without any config changes.

detail.whatUserSees: A dialog asks whether to trust this workspace. Must approve to continue.
detail.whatCodeDoes: If interactive mode: workspace trust confirmation dialog displayed via TrustDialog.tsx. Checks .claude/settings.json for prior trust. Skipped entirely for --print and --bare modes.
detail.insight: Trust is per-directory-path, not per-project. Moving a project to a new path requires re-trusting, which prevents symlink attacks that redirect to malicious CLAUDE.md files.

detail.whatUserSees: Error message with instructions to run `/login` or set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
detail.whatCodeDoes: Prints authentication error, shows login instructions, and exits with non-zero code. Does not launch the REPL.
detail.insight: The error message adapts to the platform — on macOS it suggests keychain-based OAuth, while on Linux headless servers it suggests the API key approach.

detail.whatUserSees: The interactive terminal UI appears: prompt input area, status bar, and message area
detail.whatCodeDoes: launchRepl() uses React/Ink to mount the terminal UI. Key components: REPL.tsx (root), PromptInput (input area), Messages/VirtualMessageList (chat), StatusLine (bottom bar). System prompt assembled, tools registered, MCP servers connected.
detail.insight: The entire terminal UI is a React app rendered via Ink — state management, component lifecycle, and re-rendering all work exactly like a web React app, but painting to the terminal.

detail.whatUserSees: Blinking cursor in the prompt area. Status bar shows model name, token count, permission mode.
detail.whatCodeDoes: PromptInput receives focus. System prompt fully assembled (CLAUDE.md, tool descriptions, MCP instructions, memory). All tools registered in ToolRegistry. Session ID generated.
detail.insight: Tool descriptions are included in the system prompt even before the user types anything — Claude always knows what tools are available, which is why it can proactively suggest using them.

workflows.title: Authentication
workflows.subtitle: OAuth flow from terminal to API access
workflows.userAction: Runs `/login` or prompted on first launch
workflows.userSees: Browser opens for OAuth, then terminal confirms login success

detail.whatUserSees: Either the /login command output or a first-run 'please authenticate' prompt
detail.whatCodeDoes: /login command handler or first-run detection in init.ts triggers the OAuth flow. The flow is the same regardless of trigger source.
detail.insight: The login command is a JSX component, not a plain function — it renders a live-updating terminal UI showing each step of the OAuth dance.

detail.whatUserSees: A spinner or 'Opening browser...' message appears
detail.whatCodeDoes: ConsoleOAuthFlow.tsx generates PKCE challenge (code_verifier + code_challenge). Constructs authorization URL with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and PKCE parameters.
detail.insight: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is used even though this is a local app — it prevents authorization code interception attacks if another process is listening on the same port.

detail.whatUserSees: System browser opens to Anthropic's OAuth consent page. If browser fails, URL is displayed for manual copy.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Uses open() to launch system default browser with the authorization URL. Falls back to printing the URL to terminal if browser launch fails (common in SSH sessions).
detail.insight: On headless servers without a browser, the URL is printed with a QR code-style hint — users can scan or copy the URL to authenticate from any device.

detail.whatUserSees: Terminal shows 'Waiting for authentication...' with a spinner
detail.whatCodeDoes: A local HTTP server listens on a random port for the OAuth callback. When the browser redirects back with the authorization code, the server captures it and resolves the waiting promise.
detail.insight: The callback server binds to a random high port to avoid conflicts — the port number is included in the redirect_uri registered with the OAuth provider.

detail.whatUserSees: Spinner continues briefly during token exchange
detail.whatCodeDoes: Authorization code exchanged for API token via POST to Anthropic's token endpoint. PKCE code_verifier sent to prove the same client initiated the flow.
detail.insight: The token response includes both an access token and a refresh token — the refresh token allows silent re-authentication without opening the browser again.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible change — storage happens silently
detail.whatCodeDoes: Token stored in platform-specific secure storage: macOS Keychain (via security CLI), Linux Secret Service (via libsecret), Windows Credential Manager. Fallback to encrypted file if no keyring available.
detail.insight: On macOS, the token is stored using the `security` CLI tool, not a Node.js keychain library — this avoids native module compilation issues and works in any Node.js version.

detail.whatUserSees: Green checkmark and 'Successfully logged in' message. REPL reloads.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Session reloads with new credentials. Cost state reset to zero. Feature flags refreshed from server. MCP servers re-fetched with new auth context.
detail.insight: Feature flags are fetched post-login because different account tiers (free, pro, team) unlock different capabilities — the available models and tools may change after authentication.

workflows.title: Headless / Pipe Mode
workflows.subtitle: Non-interactive queries for scripts and CI
workflows.userAction: Runs `claude -p 'prompt'` or pipes input
workflows.userSees: Answer printed to stdout, then process exits

detail.whatUserSees: No interactive UI — command runs like a typical CLI tool
detail.whatCodeDoes: main.tsx detects non-TTY stdin (piped input) or --print/-p flag. Routes to print entrypoint instead of REPL entrypoint. Supports reading prompt from stdin: `echo 'question' | claude`.
detail.insight: The TTY detection is bidirectional — both stdin and stdout are checked. If stdout is piped (e.g., `claude -p 'x' | jq`), the output format automatically switches to machine-readable JSON.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible output during initialization
detail.whatCodeDoes: entrypoints/print.ts: skips trust dialog (no interactive confirmation possible), skips REPL mount, creates QueryEngine directly. Minimal initialization path — no React/Ink overhead.
detail.insight: Headless mode skips about 60% of the initialization code — no React rendering, no keyboard handlers, no terminal UI setup. This makes it significantly faster for scripting.

detail.whatUserSees: Waiting for response — no progress indicator in pipe mode
detail.whatCodeDoes: QueryEngine processes prompt directly without interactive UI. System prompt assembled (still includes CLAUDE.md, tools). API call made. Tools execute silently without permission prompts (uses --allowedTools flag).
detail.insight: In headless mode, tool permissions must be pre-approved via --allowedTools flag — there is no way to prompt the user, so unapproved tools are simply denied.

detail.whatUserSees: Output appears in the requested format: plain text, JSON object, or streaming JSON lines
detail.whatCodeDoes: --output-format flag selects formatter: 'text' (default, just the response text), 'json' (single JSON with result, cost, duration), 'stream-json' (one JSON event per line, real-time).
detail.insight: The stream-json format emits events as they happen — tool calls, partial text, errors — making it possible to build real-time UIs on top of the headless mode.

detail.whatUserSees: Process may exit early if limits are hit
detail.whatCodeDoes: --max-turns limits the number of agent loop iterations (each tool call is one turn). --max-budget-usd sets a cost ceiling. When either limit is reached, the loop exits gracefully with partial results.
detail.insight: The budget limit checks cost after each API call, not before — so you might slightly exceed the budget on the final call. This is by design to avoid aborting mid-thought.

detail.whatUserSees: Final output printed to stdout. Process exits.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Result printed to stdout in selected format. Process exits with code 0 (success) or 1 (error/abort). Stderr used for any diagnostic messages. Session saved for potential --resume later.
detail.insight: Even headless sessions are saved — you can run `claude --resume` after a headless query to continue the conversation interactively, picking up exactly where the script left off.

workflows.title: Sending a Message
workflows.subtitle: From keystroke to streamed response
workflows.userAction: Types prompt, presses Enter
workflows.userSees: Response streams in token-by-token with tool calls inline

detail.whatUserSees: Text input area with optional @file mentions. Cursor, multi-line support, history navigation.
detail.whatCodeDoes: PromptInput.tsx captures keystrokes via useTextInput hook. Vim mode available via useVimInput. @file mentions resolved to file contents. handlePromptSubmit() called on Enter.
detail.insight: @file mentions are resolved at submit time, not when typed — so if you type @README.md and then edit README.md before pressing Enter, Claude sees the latest version.

detail.whatUserSees: If slash command: immediate response. If prompt: streaming begins.
detail.whatCodeDoes: handlePromptSubmit() checks for /command prefix. 101 commands in the lazy-loaded registry. If match found, command handler invoked. Otherwise, text sent to QueryEngine as user message.
detail.insight: Commands are lazy-loaded via dynamic import() — the 101 command handlers are not loaded into memory until first use, keeping startup time fast.

detail.whatUserSees: Command-specific output appears immediately
detail.whatCodeDoes: Command handler invoked directly. Three types: local (JS function), local-jsx (React component), prompt (forwarded to Claude).
detail.insight: Prompt-type slash commands just inject text into the conversation — /review literally sends a pre-written review instruction as if the user typed it.

detail.whatUserSees: A brief pause, then response text begins streaming in
detail.whatCodeDoes: QueryEngine.submitMessage() returns an async generator. processUserInput() resolves @mentions and attachments. recordTranscript() persists the user message BEFORE the API call for crash recovery.
detail.insight: Messages are persisted to disk before the API call — if Claude Code crashes mid-response, the user's message is preserved and can be replayed on resume.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible change — happens behind the scenes before the API call
detail.whatCodeDoes: getSystemPrompt() + getUserContext() + getSystemContext() concatenated. Includes: base identity, CLAUDE.md contents, tool descriptions, MCP server instructions, memory entries, active skills. Can exceed 10K tokens.
detail.insight: The system prompt is rebuilt for every API call, not cached — because CLAUDE.md files, MCP tools, and memory can change between messages.

detail.whatUserSees: Text appears token-by-token in the chat area, like watching someone type very fast
detail.whatCodeDoes: queryModelWithStreaming() sends the full message history to Claude API via SSE (Server-Sent Events). Each token is yielded through the async generator chain back to the React UI for incremental rendering.
detail.insight: The streaming response is parsed incrementally — content_block_delta events arrive every few tokens, and each is immediately rendered. There is no buffering delay.

detail.whatUserSees: Either the response ends (cursor returns to prompt) or a tool card appears
detail.whatCodeDoes: API response stop_reason determines next step: 'end_turn' means Claude is done, 'tool_use' means a tool call follows. This is the ReAct loop: reason (text) then act (tool) then observe (result) then repeat.
detail.insight: Claude can interleave text and tool calls freely — a single response might contain explanatory text, then a tool call, then more text. The parser handles this mixed content natively.

detail.whatUserSees: Tool card shows: tool name, parameters, real-time output (for Bash), then result
detail.whatCodeDoes: StreamingToolExecutor with readers-writers concurrency model. runToolUse() routes to the specific Tool class. Read tools (Grep, Glob, Read) run in parallel; write tools (Bash, Edit, Write) acquire exclusive lock.
detail.insight: Read tools run in parallel but write tools are exclusive — this means Claude can grep 5 files simultaneously, but file writes are serialized to prevent conflicts.

detail.whatUserSees: Complete response visible. Cursor back in prompt area. Token count updated in status bar.
detail.whatCodeDoes: VirtualMessageList renders the complete message chain. Markdown formatted and syntax-highlighted. Cost tracker updates with tokens consumed. Session auto-saved to disk.
detail.insight: VirtualMessageList only renders messages visible in the viewport — in a 500-message conversation, only ~20 messages are actually in the React tree at any time.

workflows.title: Watching a Tool Run
workflows.subtitle: Real-time tool execution with streaming output
workflows.userAction: Sees Claude decide to call a tool
workflows.userSees: A tool card expands showing name, parameters, and live output

detail.whatUserSees: A bordered card appears showing the tool name (e.g., 'Bash') and what Claude intends to do
detail.whatCodeDoes: API response contains a tool_use content block with tool name and JSON input. ToolUseCard.tsx renders immediately with tool name and intent description.
detail.insight: The tool card renders before the tool executes — users see what Claude wants to do and can cancel before any side effects occur.

detail.whatUserSees: Multiple read tools may execute simultaneously; write tools execute one at a time
detail.whatCodeDoes: Manages tool concurrency via addTool() queue. Implements readers-writers lock: read tools (Grep, Glob, Read, ListDir) acquire shared lock and run in parallel; write tools (Bash, Edit, Write) acquire exclusive lock.
detail.insight: The concurrency model is inspired by database isolation levels — read tools are like SELECT (shared locks) and write tools are like UPDATE (exclusive locks). This maximizes throughput while preventing conflicts.

detail.whatUserSees: Either the tool runs immediately or a permission dialog appears
detail.whatCodeDoes: canUseTool() checks permission rules in cascade: deny rules → allow rules → classifier (in auto mode) → ask user. The mode (ask/auto/deny/bypass) determines the fallback behavior.
detail.insight: Permission checks happen after the tool card renders but before execution — this is why you see the tool name and parameters before the permission prompt appears.

detail.whatUserSees: Dialog shows tool details and proposed action. Options: approve (y/Enter), deny (n/Esc), or approve always.
detail.whatCodeDoes: PermissionRequest.tsx renders tool details, proposed action description. User approves (y or Enter), denies (n or Esc), or saves a persistent rule. Decision stored in permission state.
detail.insight: Pressing Enter with no input defaults to 'approve' — this is a deliberate UX choice to reduce friction, since most tool calls are benign.

detail.whatUserSees: For Bash: real-time stdout/stderr streaming in the tool card. For file ops: diff view. For search: result list.
detail.whatCodeDoes: toolExecution.ts runToolUse() routes to specific Tool class (BashTool, FileWriteTool, FileReadTool, etc.). Bash output streams via child_process stdout/stderr pipes. File operations show before/after diffs.
detail.insight: BashTool output is streamed line-by-line via Node.js child_process — the terminal UI updates in real-time, so you can watch long-running commands (npm install, tests) execute live.

detail.whatUserSees: Tool card finalizes: green check (success), red X (error), orange (rejected), or gray (canceled)
detail.whatCodeDoes: UserToolResultMessage renders with status-colored indicator. Success results show output. Errors show error message. Diff view for file operations shows exactly what changed.
detail.insight: Tool results are truncated if they exceed ~100K characters — very large outputs (like `cat` on a huge file) are trimmed with a note, preventing context window overflow.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude processes the tool result and either responds with text, calls another tool, or finishes
detail.whatCodeDoes: Tool result sent back to Claude API as tool_result message. queryLoop continues with the updated conversation. Claude decides next action based on the result.
detail.insight: The entire tool result becomes part of the conversation history — Claude can reference previous tool outputs in later messages, building on accumulated context.

workflows.title: Permission Decision
workflows.subtitle: The deny/allow/ask cascade that guards every tool
workflows.userAction: Sees permission dialog, approves or denies
workflows.userSees: Permission prompt with tool details and approve/deny options

detail.whatUserSees: Tool card appears, then a permission prompt slides in below it
detail.whatCodeDoes: canUseTool() called before every single tool execution. Receives tool name, input parameters, and current conversation context.
detail.insight: Every tool call goes through permission checking, even built-in read tools — the check is fast (microseconds for allowed tools) but never skipped.

detail.whatUserSees: If denied by rule: tool card shows 'Denied' immediately, no prompt
detail.whatCodeDoes: 8 rule sources checked in priority order: CLI flags (highest), managed/enterprise settings, project .claude/settings.json, user ~/.claude/settings.json, environment defaults. Any deny match = immediate deny.
detail.insight: Deny rules are checked before allow rules — this means an enterprise admin can deny a tool that a user has allowed. Security always wins over convenience.

detail.whatUserSees: If allowed by rule: tool executes silently without any prompt
detail.whatCodeDoes: If no deny rule matched, check allow rules from same 8 sources. If any allow rule matches the tool name and parameters, the tool is approved without user interaction.
detail.insight: Allow rules support glob patterns — you can allow 'Bash(npm *)' to approve all npm commands while still prompting for other shell commands.

detail.whatUserSees: Depends on mode: auto may approve silently, ask always prompts, deny always blocks
detail.whatCodeDoes: PermissionMode determines fallback: 'auto' routes to transcript classifier, 'ask' shows interactive prompt, 'deny' rejects immediately, 'bypass' approves everything.
detail.insight: The 'bypass' mode exists but is intentionally undocumented — it is only meant for automated testing and CI pipelines, never for interactive use.

detail.whatUserSees: Tool either runs silently (classifier approved) or falls through to user prompt
detail.whatCodeDoes: Transcript classifier evaluates tool call safety based on conversation context and tool parameters. Uses a lightweight model call to classify as safe/unsafe. Trained on usage patterns.
detail.insight: The classifier sees the full conversation, not just the tool call — it can approve 'rm file.txt' if Claude just created that file, but deny it if the file was user-owned.

detail.whatUserSees: Permission dialog: tool name, parameters, explanation. Options: approve once (y), approve always (save rule), deny (n).
detail.whatCodeDoes: PermissionRequest.tsx displays tool details with syntax-highlighted parameters. Three response options: approve once, save allow rule (persisted), deny. Keyboard shortcuts: y/Enter = approve, n/Esc = deny.
detail.insight: The 'approve always' option creates a persistent allow rule in .claude/settings.json — this rule survives across sessions and is checked in the allow-rules phase next time.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible tracking — happens silently in background
detail.whatCodeDoes: Decision logged for analytics (approve/deny counts). Denial count tracked per session. Persistent rules saved to settings file if user chose 'always'.
detail.insight: Denial patterns are tracked but not acted on automatically — the system does not escalate or change behavior based on denial counts, unlike some security systems.

detail.whatUserSees: Tool executes (green) or shows denied (orange). Chat continues.
detail.whatCodeDoes: If approved: tool executes normally, result returned to agent loop. If denied: ToolDeniedError returned as tool_result, Claude sees the denial and adapts its approach.
detail.insight: When a tool is denied, Claude receives the denial as a tool result — it can then try an alternative approach, like using a different tool or asking the user for help.

workflows.title: Slash Commands
workflows.subtitle: 101 commands dispatched by type
workflows.userAction: Types `/compact`, `/help`, `/model`, etc.
workflows.userSees: Typeahead suggestions while typing, then command output

detail.whatUserSees: Typing / triggers a dropdown of matching commands with fuzzy search. Arrow keys to navigate, Enter to select.
detail.whatCodeDoes: PromptInput detects / prefix and triggers typeahead. Fuzzy matching against 101 registered command names. Suggestions rendered via CommandSuggestions.tsx overlay.
detail.insight: The fuzzy matching is forgiving — typing /comp matches /compact, /compare, and /config. The ranking considers both match quality and command frequency.

detail.whatUserSees: Brief flash as command loads (usually imperceptible)
detail.whatCodeDoes: commands.ts registry: each command has name, type, aliases, description, and a load() function. Commands are lazy-loaded via dynamic import() — only the selected command's module is loaded into memory.
detail.insight: Commands support aliases — /q is an alias for /quit, /cls for /clear. The alias system means common shortcuts work without separate implementations.

detail.whatUserSees: Different commands produce different kinds of output: text, interactive UI, or Claude response
detail.whatCodeDoes: Three command types determine execution path: 'local' (synchronous JS function), 'local-jsx' (React component rendered in REPL), 'prompt' (text sent to Claude as user message).
detail.insight: The three types exist because some commands need UI (settings panel), some need Claude (code review), and some are instant (clear screen). One interface serves all three.

detail.whatUserSees: Instant result — e.g., /compact shows 'Compacting...' then 'Done'
detail.whatCodeDoes: Direct JS function call. Examples: /compact runs compaction immediately, /clear resets message display, /cost shows accumulated costs. Returns result synchronously or as a resolved promise.
detail.insight: Local commands can modify internal state directly — /compact actually mutates the message array, which is why it is so much faster than sending a compaction request through the normal message flow.

detail.whatUserSees: Interactive panel appears — e.g., /config shows a settings menu with arrow key navigation
detail.whatCodeDoes: Dynamic import of React/Ink component. Component mounted in REPL alongside chat. Examples: /config renders ConfigPanel, /login renders ConsoleOAuthFlow, /mcp renders MCP management UI.
detail.insight: JSX command components are full React components with state, effects, and keyboard handlers — /config is essentially a mini-app running inside the terminal.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude responds as if the user typed a specific instruction
detail.whatCodeDoes: Pre-written text injected as a user message. Examples: /review sends code review instructions, /explain sends explanation request. Claude processes it through the normal query pipeline.
detail.insight: Prompt-type commands are a UX shortcut, not a special feature — they literally prepend text to the conversation. Users could type the same text manually with identical results.

detail.whatUserSees: Command output shown in chat. Interactive components close when done. Chat continues normally.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Local commands return text displayed inline. JSX components unmount on completion. Prompt commands trigger normal streaming response. All paths return to the normal REPL input state.
detail.insight: After any slash command, the REPL returns to exactly the same state — no command can permanently alter the input mode. This makes slash commands safe to experiment with.

workflows.title: Interrupting Claude
workflows.subtitle: Ctrl+C cancellation and graceful abort
workflows.userAction: Presses Ctrl+C during response
workflows.userSees: Streaming stops, partial response kept, cursor returns to prompt

detail.whatUserSees: Streaming text stops immediately. Status bar shows interrupted state.
detail.whatCodeDoes: useExitOnCtrlCD.ts handles the keypress. First Ctrl+C triggers interrupt (cancels current operation). Second rapid Ctrl+C triggers exit (terminates process).
detail.insight: The double-tap pattern is borrowed from Node.js REPL conventions — single Ctrl+C cancels the current line, double Ctrl+C exits. Users familiar with node/python REPLs feel at home.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible change — the abort propagates internally in milliseconds
detail.whatCodeDoes: AbortController.abort() fires, propagating through the async generator chain. Every yield point in the streaming pipeline checks the abort signal, making cancellation responsive at any depth.
detail.insight: The abort signal is wired through every layer — API client, tool executor, and streaming pipeline all share the same AbortController, so one Ctrl+C cancels everything simultaneously.

detail.whatUserSees: Text stops appearing. If a tool was running (e.g., Bash command), its output also stops.
detail.whatCodeDoes: API SSE connection closed. StreamingToolExecutor cancels pending tools. In-flight Bash commands receive SIGTERM. Read operations abort cleanly. Write operations may complete to avoid partial writes.
detail.insight: Write tools like Edit may complete their current operation even after Ctrl+C — aborting mid-write could leave files in a corrupted state, so they finish the atomic operation first.

detail.whatUserSees: Partial response text remains visible in the chat. Tool results already received are shown.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Messages received before abort are kept in the conversation history. Partial text blocks are preserved. Incomplete tool results may be discarded or kept depending on whether they reached a consistent state.
detail.insight: The partial response is included when sending the next message — Claude sees what it already said and can continue from that point or take a different direction.

detail.whatUserSees: If single Ctrl+C: cursor returns to prompt. If double: process exits with resume hint.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Single Ctrl+C: return to input state, user can type new message or re-ask. Double Ctrl+C (within ~500ms): triggers gracefulShutdown() which runs the full exit sequence.
detail.insight: The 500ms window for double-tap is deliberately generous — even on high-latency SSH connections, the second Ctrl+C registers. On local terminals, it feels instant.

detail.whatUserSees: Prompt cursor active. Partial response visible above. Token count reflects tokens consumed so far.
detail.whatCodeDoes: REPL returns to input state. Conversation history includes partial response. User can send new message, which will include the partial response as context for Claude.
detail.insight: Interrupting and then sending 'continue' is a valid workflow — Claude sees its partial response and picks up exactly where it left off.

detail.whatUserSees: 'Resume this session with: claude --resume SESSION_ID' printed before exit
detail.whatCodeDoes: gracefulShutdown() runs: terminal reset, session save, hook execution, analytics flush. Process exits with the resume hint message.
detail.insight: Even an interrupted session is fully resumable — the session is saved during the graceful shutdown, so --resume picks up exactly where Ctrl+C+C left off.

workflows.title: Context Compaction
workflows.subtitle: Keeping conversations alive when context fills up
workflows.userAction: Types `/compact` or auto-triggered at ~95% context
workflows.userSees: Brief pause, then 'Conversation compacted' — token count drops

detail.whatUserSees: Token counter in status bar changes color: green → yellow → orange → red as context fills up
detail.whatCodeDoes: StatusLine.tsx shows token percentage. TokenCounter watches conversation size. Warning displayed at 85%. Auto-compact threshold at ~95%.
detail.insight: The token counter uses tiktoken for accurate counting — it is not an estimate. The percentage is exact relative to the model's context window size.

detail.whatUserSees: Status bar color reflects urgency level
detail.whatCodeDoes: autoCompact.ts calculateTokenWarningState(): <70% green (healthy), 70-85% info (growing), 85-95% warning (should compact soon), >95% critical (auto-compact triggers).
detail.insight: The 95% threshold is conservative — it leaves headroom for the compaction prompt itself, which needs context space to generate a good summary.

detail.whatUserSees: Either user explicitly ran /compact, or a notification says 'Auto-compacting conversation...'
detail.whatCodeDoes: /compact command = manual trigger. isAutoCompactEnabled() config check + threshold exceeded = automatic trigger. Both paths use identical compaction logic.
detail.insight: Auto-compaction can be disabled via /config — some users prefer manual control because compaction loses fine details that might be needed later.

detail.whatUserSees: 'Compacting conversation...' message with a spinner
detail.whatCodeDoes: compact.ts groups conversation messages chronologically. Builds a prompt asking Claude to summarize while explicitly preserving: key decisions, file paths mentioned, code patterns discussed, pending tasks, and any unresolved questions.
detail.insight: The compaction prompt specifically asks to preserve file paths and code snippets — these are the details most likely lost in naive summarization but most needed for coding tasks.

detail.whatUserSees: Spinner continues for a few seconds during summarization
detail.whatCodeDoes: A forked agent generates the summary using a separate API call. This avoids polluting the main conversation with the compaction prompt. The summary replaces old messages while keeping the most recent ones intact.
detail.insight: The summary agent uses the same model as the main conversation — this ensures the summary captures nuances that a smaller model might miss.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible change — message list looks the same (summary replaces old messages behind the scenes)
detail.whatCodeDoes: buildPostCompactMessages() creates new message array: [system prompt, summary message, recent N messages]. Files context and skills re-injected. Old messages removed from active context but preserved in session file.
detail.insight: Old messages are not deleted — they remain in the session file on disk. The --resume command can access the full pre-compaction history for forensic review.

detail.whatUserSees: 'Conversation compacted' notice. Token counter drops significantly (often from 95% to 30-40%). Status bar returns to green.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Token count recalculated on new message array. Conversation continues with summary + recent context. User can send new messages immediately. Quality of responses may change slightly due to summarization.
detail.insight: Compaction typically reduces context by 60-70% — but the quality depends heavily on conversation structure. Code-heavy conversations compact worse than discussion-heavy ones.

workflows.title: Writing CLAUDE.md
workflows.subtitle: Project instructions that shape Claude's behavior
workflows.userAction: Creates or edits CLAUDE.md file in project
workflows.userSees: No visible change — instructions silently shape Claude's next response

detail.whatUserSees: User edits a CLAUDE.md file in their editor or asks Claude to create one
detail.whatCodeDoes: File written to disk at any of several locations: project root CLAUDE.md, .claude/CLAUDE.md, .claude/rules/*.md, or .local/CLAUDE.md (git-ignored personal instructions).
detail.insight: The .local/CLAUDE.md path is git-ignored by convention — it allows personal instructions (like preferred code style) that are not shared with the team.

detail.whatUserSees: No indication of discovery — it happens transparently on each message
detail.whatCodeDoes: claudemd.ts getMemoryFiles() scans for CLAUDE.md files at multiple levels: global (/etc/claude/), user (~/.claude/), project root, .claude/ directory, .claude/rules/ glob, .local/. Closer to the working directory = higher priority.
detail.insight: CLAUDE.md files are re-discovered on every API call, not cached — if you edit CLAUDE.md mid-conversation, the changes take effect on the very next message.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible expansion — @path directives are resolved behind the scenes
detail.whatCodeDoes: @path directives in CLAUDE.md are resolved: @./relative (from file location), @~/home (from home dir), @/absolute. Circular references detected and prevented. Missing files silently ignored to avoid breaking the system.
detail.insight: The @include syntax lets you factor common instructions into shared files — a monorepo can have per-package CLAUDE.md files that all @include the root coding standards.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible change — CLAUDE.md content becomes part of Claude's system prompt
detail.whatCodeDoes: context.ts getSystemContext() includes all discovered CLAUDE.md content. Files concatenated in priority order (global first, local last). Later content gets more attention from the model due to recency bias.
detail.insight: CLAUDE.md content placed last in system prompt gets the most influence — this is why project-specific instructions override global ones even without explicit priority syntax.

detail.whatUserSees: On the next message, Claude follows CLAUDE.md instructions (e.g., code style, preferred libraries, review checklist)
detail.whatCodeDoes: System prompt now includes CLAUDE.md content. Model sees instructions as authoritative context. No explicit UI indicator that instructions are active — they are seamlessly integrated.
detail.insight: CLAUDE.md instructions compete for context window space with conversation history — very long CLAUDE.md files can reduce the effective conversation length.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude consistently follows the instructions across messages and sessions
detail.whatCodeDoes: CLAUDE.md files persist on disk. Discovered fresh on each message. Changes take effect without restart. Instructions carry across sessions (since the file is re-read, not cached in session state).
detail.insight: CLAUDE.md is the most impactful configuration mechanism — it can change Claude's personality, coding style, and tool usage patterns more effectively than any settings file.

workflows.title: Changing Settings
workflows.subtitle: Interactive configuration with layered sources
workflows.userAction: Runs `/config` to open settings panel
workflows.userSees: Interactive settings menu with arrow key navigation

detail.whatUserSees: A full-screen settings panel replaces the chat view
detail.whatCodeDoes: commands/config/config.tsx is a local-jsx command that renders an interactive settings panel using React/Ink components.
detail.insight: The config panel is a React component, not a separate process — it runs inside the same Ink renderer as the chat, which is why transitions are instant.

detail.whatUserSees: Settings panel shows current values with indicators showing where each value came from
detail.whatCodeDoes: settings.ts getInitialSettings() merges from 5 sources in priority order: CLI args (highest), MDM/enterprise policy, user config (~/.claude/config.json), environment variables, built-in defaults (lowest).
detail.insight: Each setting tracks its source — the UI can show 'set by enterprise policy' vs 'set by user' vs 'default', and enterprise settings are visibly locked.

detail.whatUserSees: Arrow keys navigate categories and settings. Enter toggles or opens editors. ESC goes back.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Categories: model, permissions, theme, privacy/telemetry, effort level, etc. Each setting has a type (toggle, select, text) and validation rules. MDM-locked settings shown as read-only.
detail.insight: Enterprise (MDM) settings are displayed but grayed out with a lock icon — users can see what their admin configured but cannot override it.

detail.whatUserSees: Changes save instantly with a brief confirmation flash
detail.whatCodeDoes: Changes validated against JSON schema. User-scope settings saved to ~/.claude/config.json. Project-scope settings saved to .claude/settings.json. Invalid values rejected with error message.
detail.insight: Project settings (.claude/settings.json) are meant to be committed to git — they configure Claude for the whole team. User settings are personal preferences.

detail.whatUserSees: Changes visible immediately: theme changes repaint, model name updates in status bar
detail.whatCodeDoes: Settings emitted via event system. Components subscribed to settings changes re-render. Some settings (theme, model) apply instantly. Others (permissions) apply on next tool call.
detail.insight: No restart is ever needed for settings changes — the event system ensures all components pick up changes immediately, even mid-conversation.

detail.whatUserSees: Settings panel closes, chat resumes. Settings persist across sessions.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Config files written to disk. Changes survive process restart. Managed/MDM settings cannot be overridden by user. Complete settings visible via /config output.
detail.insight: Settings files are plain JSON — power users often edit them directly instead of using /config, especially for batch configuration of multiple settings.

workflows.title: Switching Models
workflows.subtitle: Changing the AI model mid-session
workflows.userAction: Runs `/model` or presses Meta+P
workflows.userSees: Model picker overlay, then status bar updates with new model name

detail.whatUserSees: A model picker overlay appears with a list of available models
detail.whatCodeDoes: /model command or Meta+P keyboard shortcut opens ModelSelector component. Available models fetched from config and filtered by account permissions.
detail.insight: Meta+P is inspired by VS Code's command palette shortcut — the muscle memory transfers directly from IDE users.

detail.whatUserSees: List shows: Sonnet (fast, balanced), Opus (powerful, slower), Haiku (fastest, compact). Current model highlighted.
detail.whatCodeDoes: ModelSelector.tsx renders available models with descriptions, speed indicators, and context window sizes. Current model pre-selected. Arrow keys to navigate, Enter to select.
detail.insight: The available models depend on your account tier and API key — enterprise accounts may have access to different models than consumer accounts.

detail.whatUserSees: Model picker closes. A brief flash confirms the switch.
detail.whatCodeDoes: getDefaultMainLoopModel() updated with new model selection. Session state records the model change. No conversation reset — full message history preserved for the new model.
detail.insight: Switching models mid-conversation means the new model sees the old model's responses — this works because all Claude models share the same message format.

detail.whatUserSees: Next response comes from the new model — may be faster/slower, more/less detailed
detail.whatCodeDoes: queryModelWithStreaming() routes to the new model endpoint. Previous conversation history sent as-is. Token counting adjusts for the new model's tokenizer and context window.
detail.insight: Context window sizes differ between models — switching from Opus (200K) to Haiku (200K) preserves all context, but cost-per-token changes dramatically.

detail.whatUserSees: StatusLine shows new model name. Token counting and cost display adjusted.
detail.whatCodeDoes: StatusLine.tsx updates model indicator. Cost tracker adjusts pricing for new model. Session metadata records model switch timestamp for analytics.
detail.insight: You can switch models multiple times in one conversation — some users start with Haiku for quick questions, switch to Opus for complex reasoning, then back to Sonnet for implementation.

workflows.title: Permission Mode Cycling
workflows.subtitle: Quick-switching between ask, auto, and deny
workflows.userAction: Presses Shift+Tab to cycle permission modes
workflows.userSees: Status bar indicator changes to show current permission mode

detail.whatUserSees: Status bar permission indicator changes immediately
detail.whatCodeDoes: useTextInput.ts handles the Shift+Tab keybinding. Calls cyclePermissionMode() which advances the mode to the next in sequence.
detail.insight: Shift+Tab was chosen because Tab is used for autocomplete — the Shift modifier makes it adjacent but distinct, fitting the convention of Shift+key for 'meta' actions.

detail.whatUserSees: Mode rotates through three states with distinct icons/colors
detail.whatCodeDoes: PermissionMode.ts cycles: ask (prompt for every tool) → auto (classifier decides) → deny (deny all tools) → ask. Each mode completely changes how the permission cascade behaves.
detail.insight: There is no 'allow all' in the cycle — the bypass mode exists internally but is deliberately excluded from the UI cycle to prevent accidental unrestricted execution.

detail.whatUserSees: No immediate visible change beyond the status indicator — effect seen on next tool call
detail.whatCodeDoes: 'ask' mode: every tool prompts the user. 'auto' mode: transcript classifier evaluates safety. 'deny' mode: all tools immediately denied. The mode is checked in the permission cascade's fallback step.
detail.insight: Even in 'deny' mode, explicit allow rules still work — deny mode only affects the fallback, so tools you have permanently approved continue to function.

detail.whatUserSees: Permission mode indicator changes: shield icon for ask, lightning for auto, lock for deny
detail.whatCodeDoes: StatusLine.tsx re-renders with new permission mode. Visual indicator provides immediate feedback. Color coding: yellow (ask), green (auto), red (deny).
detail.insight: The color coding is deliberate: green for auto (go fast), yellow for ask (caution), red for deny (stop). It mirrors traffic light conventions universally understood.

detail.whatUserSees: Next tool call behaves according to the new mode. Mode resets to default on new session.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Next tool call uses new permission behavior. Mode persists for the current session only (not across sessions unless saved via /config). Default mode on fresh session is 'ask'.
detail.insight: Permission mode is deliberately session-scoped — this prevents users from accidentally leaving 'deny' mode on and being confused in their next session.

workflows.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
workflows.subtitle: Customizable keybindings with Vim support
workflows.userAction: Uses shortcuts or runs `/keybindings`
workflows.userSees: Keybinding editor showing current and customizable shortcuts

detail.whatUserSees: Keybinding editor opens showing all current bindings organized by category
detail.whatCodeDoes: /keybindings command opens an interactive editor. Shows current bindings with their default values and any user overrides.
detail.insight: The keybinding editor itself uses keybindings — you navigate it with the same shortcuts you are editing, which is a fun self-referential UX.

detail.whatUserSees: Default shortcuts shown: Enter=submit, Ctrl+C=interrupt, Ctrl+R=history, Escape=cancel, Tab=accept, Meta+P=model
detail.whatCodeDoes: defaultBindings.ts defines all built-in keybindings. Some are non-rebindable (Ctrl+C for interrupt, Ctrl+D for exit) for safety. Others can be freely reassigned.
detail.insight: Ctrl+C and Ctrl+D are permanently bound — this is a safety measure ensuring users can always interrupt or exit, even if other bindings are misconfigured.

detail.whatUserSees: User selects a binding, presses new key combination, sees it updated
detail.whatCodeDoes: User can rebind any non-protected key. Custom bindings saved to ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Supports chord bindings (e.g., Ctrl+K Ctrl+C). Conflicts detected and warned.
detail.insight: Chord bindings work like VS Code — Ctrl+K followed by another key. This doubles the available shortcut space without requiring obscure modifier combinations.

detail.whatUserSees: Mode indicator shows INSERT or NORMAL. Escape toggles. Vi navigation (hjkl) in NORMAL mode.
detail.whatCodeDoes: useVimInput.ts implements a subset of Vim: INSERT mode (default typing) ↔ NORMAL mode (Escape). i/a/o enter INSERT. hjkl navigation in NORMAL. Visual mode for selection. Enabled via /config or settings.
detail.insight: The Vim emulation covers about 40% of Vim's commands — enough for comfortable editing but not a full implementation. Advanced Vim users may miss text objects and macros.

detail.whatUserSees: Custom bindings take effect immediately. Listed in help menu and keybinding editor.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Custom bindings take effect immediately without restart. Persisted in ~/.claude/keybindings.json across sessions. Displayed in /help and /keybindings output.
detail.insight: Keybindings are loaded at startup and also watched for changes — editing the JSON file directly while Claude is running will pick up changes on the next keypress.

workflows.title: Setting Up MCP Servers
workflows.subtitle: Connecting external tools via Model Context Protocol
workflows.userAction: Runs `/mcp` or edits .claude/mcp.json
workflows.userSees: MCP management panel showing connected servers and available tools

detail.whatUserSees: Either the /mcp management panel or direct editing of mcp.json config file
detail.whatCodeDoes: /mcp command opens management UI. Alternatively, users edit ~/.claude/mcp.json (user scope) or .claude/mcp.json (project scope) directly.
detail.insight: Project-scope mcp.json (.claude/mcp.json) is meant to be committed to git — it configures team-wide MCP servers that everyone on the project should have.

detail.whatUserSees: Server list populates with configured servers and their status
detail.whatCodeDoes: services/mcp/config.ts parseMcpConfig(): reads user config, project config, and enterprise configs. Merges server definitions. Each server has: command, args, env, and optional authentication.
detail.insight: MCP configs from all three scopes are merged, not overridden — a project can add servers to the user's base set, and enterprise policy can add mandatory servers.

detail.whatUserSees: Brief loading indicator as servers are discovered and checked
detail.whatCodeDoes: officialRegistry.ts prefetches the official MCP server list (known trusted servers). Local servers found via config files. Each server validated for accessibility before listing.
detail.insight: The official registry is a curated list of vetted MCP servers — connecting to a registered server skips the approval dialog, reducing friction for trusted tools.

detail.whatUserSees: If new/untrusted server: approval dialog shows server details. If known: auto-approved.
detail.whatCodeDoes: First-time connection to non-registry server requires user approval via MCPServerApprovalDialog.tsx. Shows server name, command it will run, capabilities requested, and potential permissions.
detail.insight: MCP server approval is per-server-command — changing the server's command (even slightly) triggers re-approval, preventing config injection attacks.

detail.whatUserSees: Dialog showing server name, what command it runs, and what capabilities it provides. Approve or deny.
detail.whatCodeDoes: MCPServerApprovalDialog.tsx renders server details. User approves or denies. Approval persisted so the dialog does not appear again for the same server configuration.
detail.insight: The dialog shows the exact command that will be spawned — users can inspect whether a server runs a trusted binary or a suspicious script.

detail.whatUserSees: Server status changes from 'connecting' to 'connected'. Tool count displayed.
detail.whatCodeDoes: client.ts getMcpToolsCommandsAndResources(): spawns server process, establishes JSON-RPC connection, fetches available tools/resources/prompts. Tools registered in the tool system alongside built-in tools.
detail.insight: MCP servers are child processes — they run locally on the user's machine. The JSON-RPC protocol means any language can implement an MCP server, not just JavaScript.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude can now use the MCP server's tools. They appear naturally in responses alongside built-in tools.
detail.whatCodeDoes: MCPTool.ts handles execution routing for MCP tools. Tool descriptions included in system prompt. ListMcpResourceTool/ReadMcpResourceTool provide resource access. Persists across sessions via config file.
detail.insight: MCP tools are indistinguishable from built-in tools in the API call — Claude does not know whether a tool is built-in or MCP-provided, it just uses the best tool for the job.

workflows.title: Installing Skills & Plugins
workflows.subtitle: Extending Claude Code with new commands
workflows.userAction: Runs `/skills` or `/plugin install`
workflows.userSees: Skills list or plugin installation progress

detail.whatUserSees: /skills shows available and installed skills with descriptions. /plugin install prompts for plugin name.
detail.whatCodeDoes: /skills lists available skills from bundled and user directories. /plugin install adds new plugin. /plugin list shows installed plugins with their versions and capabilities.
detail.insight: Skills and plugins are conceptually different: skills are SKILL.md files (instructions for Claude), while plugins are executable code (new tools and commands).

detail.whatUserSees: List of available skills with names, descriptions, and activation triggers
detail.whatCodeDoes: loadSkillsDir.ts scans ~/.claude/skills/ for SKILL.md files. getBundledSkills() returns skills that ship with Claude Code. getDynamicSkills() loads runtime-discovered skills from MCP servers and plugins.
detail.insight: SKILL.md files are just markdown with a specific header format — anyone can create a skill by writing a markdown file with instructions, no coding required.

detail.whatUserSees: Plugin install progress bar, then confirmation with added capabilities listed
detail.whatCodeDoes: services/plugins/: plugin manifest validation against schema, installation to ~/.claude/plugins/, command and tool registration. Plugins can add new tools, slash commands, or skills.
detail.insight: Plugins run in the same process as Claude Code — there is no sandbox isolation. This is why plugin installation requires explicit user approval.

detail.whatUserSees: New commands appear in typeahead. New tools available to Claude immediately.
detail.whatCodeDoes: New commands from skills/plugins registered in the command registry (commands.ts). New tools registered in toolRegistry. Available immediately without restart.
detail.insight: Skills and plugins can register commands that shadow built-in ones — a plugin could override /compact with a custom implementation, though this is discouraged.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude naturally uses skill knowledge when relevant, without explicit invocation
detail.whatCodeDoes: Skills appear in system prompt context. SKILL.md content injected when the skill's activation trigger matches the current conversation context. Skills are contextually loaded, not always present.
detail.insight: Skills are injected based on conversation context — a 'database migration' skill activates when Claude detects migration-related discussion, without the user explicitly invoking it.

detail.whatUserSees: New /commands available in typeahead. New tools available to Claude. Listed in /skills and /plugin list.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Extensions persist across sessions via config files and installation directories. Listed in /skills output (skills) and /plugin list (plugins). Removable via /plugin uninstall.
detail.insight: The extension system is intentionally simple — no package registry, no version resolution, no dependency hell. Each extension is self-contained.

workflows.title: Sub-Agent Spawning
workflows.subtitle: Claude delegates work to child agents
workflows.userAction: Watches Claude use AgentTool for parallel work
workflows.userSees: Nested agent card showing sub-task progress

detail.whatUserSees: Claude mentions it will work on sub-tasks in parallel, then an AgentTool card appears
detail.whatCodeDoes: The agent loop determines that the current task benefits from decomposition. Calls AgentTool (also called 'Task' tool) with a sub-task description, passing relevant context.
detail.insight: Claude decides when to spawn sub-agents on its own — there is no explicit command. It learns from CLAUDE.md instructions and conversation context when parallelism is beneficial.

detail.whatUserSees: Sub-agent card shows either 'forked' (shares context) or 'spawned' (fresh context) indicator
detail.whatCodeDoes: Fork mode: child receives parent's conversation prefix (faster startup, shared context, cheaper). Spawn mode: fresh QueryEngine with clean context (full isolation, no parent state leakage).
detail.insight: Fork mode copies the parent's message history, so the child already knows what has been discussed — this is dramatically cheaper than spawn mode, which starts from scratch.

detail.whatUserSees: Sub-agent card shows the task description and a 'working...' indicator
detail.whatCodeDoes: New QueryEngine instance created with its own message history and system prompt. System prompt tailored to the specific sub-task. Separate AbortController for independent cancellation.
detail.insight: Each child QueryEngine has its own token budget and cost tracking — a runaway sub-agent does not consume the parent's context window.

detail.whatUserSees: If code changes needed: 'Creating worktree...' message. If read-only: no worktree message.
detail.whatCodeDoes: If the sub-task involves code changes: git worktree create makes an isolated branch. The child agent works in the worktree directory. Read-only tasks use the same working directory as parent.
detail.insight: Git worktrees let multiple agents edit code simultaneously without conflicts — each works on a separate branch in a separate directory, then results are merged.

detail.whatUserSees: Sub-agent card shows streaming progress — tool calls, text output, nested within the parent card
detail.whatCodeDoes: Child enters its own queryLoop. Can use all tools, call API, and even spawn grandchildren. Runs independently of parent. Output streamed to parent's UI via nested rendering.
detail.insight: Sub-agents can spawn their own sub-agents — the system supports arbitrary nesting depth, though in practice more than 2 levels deep is rare and usually indicates over-decomposition.

detail.whatUserSees: Sub-agent card finalizes with a summary of what was accomplished
detail.whatCodeDoes: Child completes → final result returned to parent as tool_result message. If worktree was used: changes available for merge (parent can git merge or cherry-pick). Child's context is discarded after result extraction.
detail.insight: Only the sub-agent's final result is returned to the parent — the full conversation history of the child is discarded, keeping the parent's context clean.

detail.whatUserSees: Parent agent processes sub-task results and continues with the main task
detail.whatCodeDoes: Parent agent receives sub-task results as tool_result. Decides next action: more sub-tasks, direct work, or final response. Multiple children can run and complete in any order.
detail.insight: Sub-agents are the key to Claude Code's ability to handle large tasks — a 'refactor this module' request might spawn separate agents for each file, working in parallel.

workflows.title: IDE Integration
workflows.subtitle: Connecting Claude Code to VS Code or JetBrains
workflows.userAction: Launches with `--ide` or auto-detected
workflows.userSees: IDE status indicator and bi-directional file sync

detail.whatUserSees: Claude Code terminal shows 'Connected to VS Code' or similar IDE indicator
detail.whatCodeDoes: --ide flag explicitly enables IDE bridge. Auto-detection checks for running VS Code or JetBrains instances via environment variables and process inspection. useDirectConnect.ts establishes the communication channel.
detail.insight: IDE detection works by checking environment variables that IDEs set in their integrated terminals — VS Code sets TERM_PROGRAM=vscode, making detection reliable.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible setup — bridge established silently in background
detail.whatCodeDoes: Bidirectional communication channel between terminal Claude Code and IDE extension. Uses local IPC (named pipes on Windows, Unix sockets elsewhere). File change events, selections, and diagnostics synced.
detail.insight: The bridge uses Unix domain sockets for near-zero-latency communication — file change events from the IDE reach Claude Code in under 1ms.

detail.whatUserSees: @file mentions auto-suggest IDE open files. Selected code in IDE visible to Claude.
detail.whatCodeDoes: useIdeAtMentioned.ts: @file mentions resolve to IDE's currently open files for auto-suggest. useIdeSelection.ts: text selected in IDE editor is sent to Claude as context. Diagnostics (errors, warnings) available.
detail.insight: When you select code in VS Code and switch to Claude Code's terminal, the selection is automatically available — Claude can reference 'the selected code' without you pasting it.

detail.whatUserSees: IdeStatusIndicator shows connection state. File changes by Claude appear in IDE instantly.
detail.whatCodeDoes: IdeStatusIndicator.tsx shows connection state (connected, disconnected, syncing). IDE receives Claude's file write/edit operations in real-time via the bridge, triggering IDE refresh.
detail.insight: File changes made by Claude trigger IDE file watchers — the IDE instantly shows the new file contents, diffs, and any new diagnostics (like TypeScript errors).

detail.whatUserSees: Seamless workflow: edit in IDE, ask Claude in terminal, see Claude's changes in IDE
detail.whatCodeDoes: Claude aware of IDE state: open files, selections, diagnostics. File operations reflected in IDE immediately. Diagnostic context (TypeScript errors, linting) available for Claude to reference.
detail.insight: The IDE bridge enables a feedback loop: Claude reads diagnostics, makes fixes, IDE re-evaluates, Claude sees if the fix worked — all without the user doing anything.

workflows.title: Resuming a Session
workflows.subtitle: Picking up where you left off
workflows.userAction: Runs `claude --resume` or `/resume`
workflows.userSees: Session picker showing recent conversations, then full history restored

detail.whatUserSees: Either a session picker list, the most recent session loading, or a specific session loading by ID
detail.whatCodeDoes: Three resume modes: `claude --resume` (interactive picker), `claude --continue` (most recent session automatically), `claude --resume UUID` (specific session by ID). Also available as /resume command in REPL.
detail.insight: The --continue flag is the fastest way to resume — it skips the picker entirely and loads the most recent session in under a second.

detail.whatUserSees: Scrollable list of recent sessions with titles, dates, message counts, and model used
detail.whatCodeDoes: ResumeConversation.tsx lists recent sessions from ~/.claude/projects/. Each entry shows: auto-generated title, timestamp, message count, model, and project path. Search/filter by keyword.
detail.insight: Session titles are auto-generated from the first user message — this makes the picker useful even if you never named your sessions.

detail.whatUserSees: Brief loading indicator as conversation history is restored
detail.whatCodeDoes: sessionStorage.ts loadTranscriptFromFile() reads the JSONL session file. All messages, tool calls, tool results, and permission decisions reconstructed from the serialized format.
detail.insight: Sessions are stored as JSONL (one JSON object per line) — this format is append-friendly (no need to rewrite the whole file on each message) and human-readable for debugging.

detail.whatUserSees: Chat history appears in the message area. Status bar shows restored session info.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Full session state restored: model selection, permission mode, tool configurations, MCP server connections re-established. Cost history loaded from session metadata. CLAUDE.md re-discovered from current directory.
detail.insight: MCP servers are reconnected on resume — if a server was available in the original session but is now offline, its tools gracefully degrade to 'unavailable'.

detail.whatUserSees: Token counter shows how much context the restored session uses. May see auto-compaction notice.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Previous messages loaded into context window. If the restored conversation exceeds the context limit, auto-compaction triggers immediately. System prompt reassembled with current CLAUDE.md content.
detail.insight: A resumed session uses current CLAUDE.md, not the CLAUDE.md from when the session was created — this means updated project instructions apply to old conversations.

detail.whatUserSees: Full chat history visible. Cursor in prompt area. Can send new messages immediately.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Chat continues from the last message. Full history visible and scrollable. Token count reflects restored context. New messages append to existing session file.
detail.insight: Resumed sessions are indistinguishable from continuous sessions — Claude sees the full history and can reference anything discussed previously.

workflows.title: Exiting & Cleanup
workflows.subtitle: Graceful shutdown with session persistence
workflows.userAction: Presses Ctrl+D twice or types `/exit`
workflows.userSees: Brief cleanup, resume hint printed, process exits

detail.whatUserSees: Input area closes. Brief pause as cleanup runs.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Multiple exit triggers: double Ctrl+D (EOF signal), /exit or /quit command, SIGINT (Ctrl+C+C), SIGTERM (kill), SIGHUP (terminal close). All converge to gracefulShutdown().
detail.insight: SIGHUP handling is critical for remote sessions — if an SSH connection drops, the session is saved before the process dies, making it fully resumable.

detail.whatUserSees: Very brief pause (usually sub-second) — cleanup happens fast
detail.whatCodeDoes: gracefulShutdown.ts runs an ordered cleanup sequence. Each step has a timeout to prevent hanging. A 5-second failsafe timeout ensures the process always exits, even if a step hangs.
detail.insight: The cleanup has a hard 5-second failsafe — if any cleanup step hangs (e.g., a stuck network request), the process force-exits after 5 seconds rather than hanging forever.

detail.whatUserSees: Terminal returns to normal state — no visual artifacts left behind
detail.whatCodeDoes: Synchronous (immediate, no timeout): disable mouse tracking sequences, exit alternate screen buffer, disable extended key reporting, disable focus event reporting, disable bracketed paste mode.
detail.insight: Terminal reset is synchronous and first — this ensures the terminal is usable even if subsequent cleanup steps fail. A corrupted terminal state is worse than lost analytics.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible indication — save happens silently within 2-second budget
detail.whatCodeDoes: 2-second timeout: save complete transcript to ~/.claude/projects/ as JSONL, persist current settings state, write memory snapshots. Session file includes all messages, tool calls, and metadata.
detail.insight: The 2-second timeout for session save is generous — most saves complete in under 100ms. The timeout exists for edge cases like slow disk I/O on network-mounted filesystems.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible indication — hooks run silently within 1.5-second budget
detail.whatCodeDoes: 1.5-second timeout: execute any registered SessionEnd hooks. These are user-configured cleanup scripts defined in .claude/settings.json. Common uses: log rotation, notification, backup.
detail.insight: SessionEnd hooks run with a tight timeout — they are meant for quick cleanup tasks, not long-running processes. A hook that takes too long is silently killed.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible indication — analytics flush happens in final 500ms
detail.whatCodeDoes: 500ms timeout: flush any buffered telemetry events to analytics servers. Profiling report logged to debug output. If flush fails (no network), events are lost — analytics are best-effort.
detail.insight: Analytics are deliberately best-effort — if the network is down, events are silently dropped. User experience is never degraded for telemetry purposes.

detail.whatUserSees: 'Resume this session with: claude --resume abc123' printed to terminal, then process exits
detail.whatCodeDoes: Print resume command with session ID to stderr (so it is visible even when stdout is piped). Process exits with code 0. 5-second failsafe timer cleared.
detail.insight: The resume hint is printed to stderr, not stdout — this means piping Claude's output (e.g., `claude -p 'x' > file`) does not include the resume hint in the file.

workflows.title: Plan Mode
workflows.subtitle: Read-only exploration before committing to changes
workflows.userAction: Claude enters plan mode to design an approach before writing code
workflows.userSees: Plan mode indicator appears; Claude explores code read-only, then presents a plan for approval

detail.whatUserSees: Plan mode banner appears. Claude starts exploring the codebase without making changes.
detail.whatCodeDoes: EnterPlanModeTool.call() sets appState.toolPermissionContext.mode = 'plan'. prepareContextForPlanMode() stashes the current permission mode as prePlanMode for later restoration. If auto-mode was active, calls stripDangerousPermissionsForAutoMode() to reduce permissions during planning.
detail.insight: Entering plan mode from auto-mode is special: dangerous permissions are stripped and stashed separately. This prevents the classifier from accidentally approving writes during what should be read-only exploration.
detail.codeLogic: The tool returns specific instructions to Claude: 'DO NOT write files yet. This is read-only exploration phase.' This is injected as a system-level constraint that shapes Claude's behavior.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude reads files, searches code, asks questions — but never writes or executes. The plan file grows as Claude documents its approach.
detail.whatCodeDoes: During plan mode, only read-only tools are available: Read, Grep, Glob, LSP, plus plan control tools (EnterPlanMode, ExitPlanMode, AskUserQuestion). Write/Edit/Bash are blocked at the permission layer — if Claude tries them, they fail before execution.
detail.insight: Plan mode doesn't just tell Claude to not write — it enforces it structurally. Even if Claude hallucinated a Bash call, the permission system would block it before execution.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude may ask clarifying questions via AskUserQuestion, or present its plan.
detail.whatCodeDoes: AskUserQuestion tool is specifically whitelisted in plan mode. Claude uses it to resolve ambiguity before committing to a plan. The plan file (at a path in .claude/plans/) is written via the one exception to read-only: plan file edits are allowed.
detail.insight: The plan file is the ONLY file Claude can write during plan mode. This is a deliberate exception — the plan needs to be persisted for user review.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude presents the plan and requests approval. User reviews and approves or requests changes.
detail.whatCodeDoes: ExitPlanModeTool called with plan + allowedPrompts. For teammates (sub-agents), sends plan_approval_request to team lead's mailbox and awaits plan_approval_response. For standalone: user must approve via terminal dialog. On approval: restores mode = prePlanMode (original permission mode). If auto-mode was stashed, calls restoreDangerousPermissions().
detail.insight: For sub-agents in a team, plan approval is asynchronous — the sub-agent sends its plan to the team lead and blocks until the lead responds via the inbox polling system. The lead can approve, reject, or modify the plan.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude begins implementing the approved plan. Full tool access restored. Step-by-step progress visible.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Permission mode restored to pre-plan state. All tools available again. Claude follows the plan file as a guide. allowedPrompts from ExitPlanMode may pre-approve certain tool categories (e.g., 'run tests', 'install dependencies').
detail.insight: allowedPrompts is a powerful mechanism: by pre-approving categories like 'run tests', the user avoids being prompted for every pytest call. This bridges the gap between plan-mode safety and execution-mode productivity.

workflows.title: System Prompt Assembly
workflows.subtitle: How 15+ fragments become one system prompt with cache optimization
workflows.userAction: Invisible — happens before every API call
workflows.userSees: Nothing directly — but the system prompt shapes every response Claude gives

detail.whatUserSees: User pressed Enter or a tool result is being sent back to Claude.
detail.whatCodeDoes: queryModelWithStreaming() in query.ts is about to call the Claude API. Before sending, it must assemble the complete system prompt from multiple sources. This happens via buildSystemPromptBlocks() in services/api/claude.ts.
detail.insight: The system prompt is not a static string — it's reassembled before every API call. Some sections are memoized (cached until /clear or /compact), but volatile sections recompute every turn.

detail.whatUserSees: No visible indicator — context gathering is sub-second.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Three async calls run in parallel: (1) getSystemPrompt() builds the base prompt, (2) getUserContext() discovers CLAUDE.md files and date, (3) getSystemContext() collects git status and cache breakers. Results merged into a single context object.
detail.insight: getUserContext() scans for CLAUDE.md files in a priority chain: /etc/claude-code/ (global, lowest) → ~/.claude/ (user) → project CLAUDE.md → .claude/CLAUDE.md → .claude/rules/*.md → CLAUDE.local.md (highest). Files loaded last get most model attention.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible.
detail.whatCodeDoes: The system prompt is built from 15+ ordered sections: (1) Attribution header, (2) Introduction ('You are an interactive agent...'), (3) System/tool permission rules, (4) Tool usage instructions, (5) Task/todo system, (6) Git instructions, (7) CLAUDE.md content, (8) Memory files, (9) Output style rules, (10) Language preference, (11) MCP tool descriptions, (12) Hook explanations, (13) System reminder instructions, (14) Advisor tool instructions, (15) Chrome instructions, then a DYNAMIC_BOUNDARY marker, then append-system-prompt.
detail.insight: Sections before __SYSTEM_PROMPT_DYNAMIC_BOUNDARY__ are memoized and globally cacheable across organizations. Sections after are session-specific. This split is critical for prompt caching — Anthropic can cache the static prefix across all users, dramatically reducing latency and cost.
detail.codeLogic: Volatile sections (marked DANGEROUS_uncachedSystemPromptSection) recompute every turn and break the prompt cache. This is intentional for features that change between turns (e.g., current timestamp) but expensive — each volatile section adds latency.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible — but this determines which tools Claude can use.
detail.whatCodeDoes: toolToAPISchema() converts each enabled tool into Anthropic API format: { name, description, input_schema }. Each tool's prompt.ts file provides a detailed description including usage guidelines, dangers, and examples. MCP tools may be deferred (defer_loading: true) if tool_search beta is active.
detail.insight: Tool descriptions are a MAJOR token consumer — 40 tools with detailed prompts can use 2000+ tokens. This is why deferred loading exists: MCP tools are announced as available but their full schemas are only loaded when the model requests them via ToolSearchTool.
detail.codeLogic: Each tool has a prompt.ts that is essentially a mini-manual for Claude. For example, BashTool's prompt includes dangerous command patterns, FileEditTool's prompt explains the diff format. These are invisible to users but critical for tool quality.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible — but this dramatically affects response latency.
detail.whatCodeDoes: splitSysPromptPrefix() splits the assembled prompt at __SYSTEM_PROMPT_DYNAMIC_BOUNDARY__. Block 1 (before boundary) gets cache_control: { scope: 'global' } — cacheable across all users. Blocks after get scope: 'session' or null. The API can serve cached prefix from CDN.
detail.insight: This is Anthropic's prompt caching optimization: the base system prompt + tool descriptions are identical for all Claude Code users, so they can be cached once globally. Only the user-specific parts (CLAUDE.md, git status) are unique per session. This can save 50-80% of input token processing.

detail.whatUserSees: Spinner appears — the request is now in flight.
detail.whatCodeDoes: The final API request includes: system prompt blocks (with cache controls), conversation messages, tool schemas, and model parameters. The cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens in the response reveal how much was cached vs freshly processed.
detail.insight: The cost tracker shows 'cache read' vs 'cache creation' tokens separately. Cache reads are 90% cheaper than fresh processing. A well-cached session can save significant cost — but every CLAUDE.md edit or /clear command breaks the cache.
detail.codeLogic: Token consumption breakdown: Base prompt ~800-1500 tokens, Tool descriptions ~500-2000 tokens, CLAUDE.md variable (<12.5k tokens typically), Git status ~100-500 tokens, MCP instructions variable. Total system prompt can easily be 3000-5000 tokens BEFORE the user's first message.

workflows.title: Tool Selection Logic
workflows.subtitle: How Claude Code decides which tools the model can see and use
workflows.userAction: Invisible — determines which tools appear in API requests
workflows.userSees: Claude uses tools like Read, Bash, Edit — but the available set is filtered by multiple layers

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible — this is internal tool registration.
detail.whatCodeDoes: getAllBaseTools() returns all registered tools: ~40 built-in tools (Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Grep, Glob, Agent, WebFetch, etc.) plus any MCP tools and plugin tools. Each tool has: name, description, input_schema, isEnabled(), and permission metadata.
detail.insight: Tools are not hardcoded — they are registered dynamically. MCP servers can add unlimited tools at runtime. The tool list can change mid-session when MCP servers connect or disconnect.

detail.whatUserSees: Some tools silently disappear depending on context.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Each tool has isEnabled() that checks feature flags, platform, and session state. Example: VoiceMode tools only enabled if VOICE_MODE feature flag is on. LSPTool only if IDE connected. NotebookEditTool only if .ipynb files detected.
detail.insight: Feature flags (from GrowthBook) can enable/disable tools remotely. Anthropic can ship a new tool to 1% of users, then gradually roll it out — without any code update on the user's machine.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — filtered tools never appear in Claude's awareness.
detail.whatCodeDoes: canUseTool() checks permission rules from 8 sources: --allowedTools/--disallowedTools CLI flags, MDM enterprise policies, project .claude/settings.json, user ~/.claude/settings.json, and built-in defaults. Deny rules checked first, then allow rules.
detail.insight: A tool denied by MDM policy cannot be overridden by the user — enterprise admins have absolute veto. This is how organizations prevent Claude from running arbitrary shell commands.

detail.whatUserSees: In plan mode, only read-only tools are available. In deny mode, no tools at all.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Current permission mode (ask/auto/deny/plan) determines the final filter: plan mode → only read-only tools + plan control tools. deny mode → all tools excluded. auto/ask → all non-denied tools included.
detail.insight: Plan mode is the most restrictive: even if a tool is explicitly allowed by user rules, it won't appear if it's a write tool. The mode filter is the final gate.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — but this determines Claude's tool awareness.
detail.whatCodeDoes: toolToAPISchema() for each surviving tool builds the Anthropic API tool format. MCP tools with tool_search beta get defer_loading: true — they're announced as available but schema not sent until model requests via ToolSearchTool. A synthetic <available-deferred-tools> message is injected.
detail.insight: Deferred loading is a clever optimization: instead of sending 50+ MCP tool schemas (thousands of tokens), only their names are listed. Claude sees '<available-deferred-tools>' and can call ToolSearchTool to fetch specific schemas on demand. This can save 5000+ tokens per request.

detail.whatUserSees: Claude never mentions or attempts to use excluded tools.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Excluded tools are simply not included in the API request. The model never sees them, so it cannot call them. This is a security boundary — the model cannot access tools it doesn't know about.
detail.insight: This is defense-in-depth: even if Claude somehow 'knew' about an excluded tool and tried to call it, the tool execution layer would reject it. But by not including it in the schema, the model doesn't even try.

workflows.title: Auto-Permission Classifier
workflows.subtitle: ML-based tool approval that reads your entire conversation
