workflows.userAction: Enables auto mode via Shift+Tab, --auto flag, or /permissions
workflows.userSees: Tools execute without asking — or occasionally get soft-denied with a warning

detail.whatUserSees: Permission mode indicator in status bar changes to 'auto'. Tools start executing without approval prompts.
detail.whatCodeDoes: autoModeState.ts sets autoModeActive = true. Feature-gated behind TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER flag. Can be enabled via CLI (--auto), settings (autoMode.enabled), or runtime toggle (Shift+Tab). GrowthBook feature gate checked first — Anthropic can circuit-break auto mode remotely.
detail.insight: Anthropic can disable auto mode for all users instantly via GrowthBook: tengu_auto_mode_config.enabled = 'disabled'. This is a safety valve — if the classifier misbehaves, it can be killed remotely without a code update.
detail.codeLogic: Three state flags: autoModeActive (current status), autoModeFlagCli (set by --auto), autoModeCircuitBroken (set by remote kill). Circuit breaker prevents re-entry even if user retries.

detail.whatUserSees: Read-only tools execute instantly. Dangerous tools may pause briefly.
detail.whatCodeDoes: isAutoModeAllowlistedTool() checks if the tool is inherently safe: FileRead, Grep, Glob, LSP, Plan tools, Task tools — all bypass the classifier entirely. Only potentially dangerous tools (Bash, FileWrite, FileEdit, WebFetch) go to the classifier.
detail.insight: The allowlist is hardcoded — read-only tools ALWAYS skip classification. This means auto mode has zero overhead for exploration tasks. Only mutation/execution tools trigger the ML classifier.

detail.whatUserSees: Brief pause (200-800ms) while classifier decides. Usually imperceptible.
detail.whatCodeDoes: classifierDecision() sends the ENTIRE session transcript + current tool call + user's autoMode rules to a separate Claude model (Sonnet/Haiku) via sideQuery. The classifier prompt (auto_mode_system_prompt.txt) instructs it to evaluate: Is this tool call safe and aligned with the user's intent?
detail.insight: The classifier sees your ENTIRE conversation — not just the current tool call. This means it understands context: a 'rm -rf' in a cleanup task is different from 'rm -rf' unprompted. But it also means every classified tool call costs additional API tokens.
detail.codeLogic: The classifier prompt has template slots: <user_allow_rules_to_replace>, <user_deny_rules_to_replace>, <user_environment_to_replace>. User's autoMode config rules are injected here. The classifier returns structured JSON: { decision: 'allow' | 'soft_deny' | 'deny', reasoning: '...' }.

detail.whatUserSees: Rules the user configured in settings affect classifier behavior.
detail.whatCodeDoes: User's autoMode settings provide three rule types: allow[] (regex patterns always permitted, e.g., 'npm test'), soft_deny[] (warned but allowed, e.g., 'rm'), environment[] (env vars/secrets to mask). These are injected into the classifier prompt, overriding default rules.
detail.insight: Users can effectively program the classifier: 'always allow npm test' means the classifier will never block test runs. This is more flexible than a simple allowlist because the classifier understands intent, not just command strings.

detail.whatUserSees: Tool executes smoothly without any prompt.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Permission granted. Tool proceeds to normal execution via StreamingToolExecutor. Decision cached in memory for the session.
detail.insight: Classifier decisions are NOT persisted across sessions — each new session starts fresh. This means the classifier's 'learning' is session-scoped, not permanent.

detail.whatUserSees: For soft_deny: warning shown but tool proceeds. For deny: tool blocked, error message shown.
detail.whatCodeDoes: soft_deny → tool executes with a warning banner. deny → tool returns error result to Claude, which may retry with different parameters or explain why it can't proceed. Denial count tracked — after repeated denials, the system may suggest switching to a different approach.
detail.insight: soft_deny is a unique middle ground: the tool runs but the user is warned. This handles cases where the classifier is uncertain — better to execute and warn than to block a legitimate operation.

workflows.title: Telemetry & Data Collection
workflows.subtitle: Dual-backend analytics: what data flows to Anthropic and Datadog
workflows.userAction: Invisible — telemetry runs in background during every session
workflows.userSees: No direct indication — telemetry is silent by design

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — events are generated silently on every action.
detail.whatCodeDoes: logEvent() or logEventAsync() in services/analytics/index.ts queues an event. Events fire on: startup, every API call, every tool use, every error, every slash command, model switches, permission decisions, OAuth flows, voice toggles, feedback surveys, and more. Over 100 distinct event types exist (prefixed 'tengu_').
detail.insight: Events queue before the analytics sink is attached — nothing is lost even during early startup. The metadata restriction is notable: ONLY booleans and numbers are allowed, no strings. This is enforced by the type AnalyticsMetadata_I_VERIFIED_THIS_IS_NOT_CODE_OR_FILEPATHS to prevent accidental code/filepath leakage.
detail.codeLogic: The type name itself is a code review signal: any developer adding string metadata must use a type named 'I_VERIFIED_THIS_IS_NOT_CODE_OR_FILEPATHS' — making it impossible to accidentally leak user code.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Every event is enriched with: sessionId, model name, userType ('ant' for Anthropic employees), platform, arch, nodeVersion, terminal, isCI, version, buildTime, rss/heapUsed memory, cpuPercent, uptime. For OAuth users: subscriptionType, accountUUID, organizationUUID. A SHA256 hash of the repo URL (first 16 chars) is included as 'rh' for server-side joining across sessions.
detail.insight: The 'rh' field is a partial SHA256 of your git remote URL — Anthropic can correlate sessions across the same repo without knowing the actual repo URL. This enables per-project usage analytics while preserving some privacy.
detail.codeLogic: Process metrics (RSS, heap, CPU) are collected on EVERY event — Anthropic monitors Claude Code's resource consumption across all users in real-time.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — routing happens internally.
detail.whatCodeDoes: sink.ts routes each event to TWO backends: (1) Datadog (64 whitelisted event types only, cardinality-reduced), (2) First-Party event logging (all events, richer context). Sampling rates are configurable per-event via GrowthBook config tengu_event_sampling_config.
detail.insight: Not all events go to Datadog — only 64 specific types are whitelisted. But ALL events go to Anthropic's first-party logging. The sampling config is controlled remotely via GrowthBook, meaning Anthropic can increase/decrease telemetry granularity without a code update.
detail.codeLogic: Datadog normalizes cardinality: MCP tool names are collapsed to just 'mcp', dev versions truncated, external user models normalized to 'other'. This prevents high-cardinality billing in Datadog while still capturing the data in 1P logging.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — Datadog is a third-party observability platform.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Events sent to https://http-intake.logs.us5.datadoghq.com/api/v2/logs with embedded client token pubbbf48e6d78dae54bceaa4acf463299bf. Batched: max 100 events per batch, flushed every 15 seconds. 5-second network timeout. User IDs hashed into 30 buckets for privacy-preserving DAU estimation.
detail.insight: Anthropic uses Datadog US5 region for real-time operational monitoring. The client token is embedded in the binary — it's not a secret (Datadog client tokens are designed to be public). User IDs are hashed into 30 buckets, so Datadog sees bucket numbers, not individual users.
detail.codeLogic: The Datadog endpoint, client token, and batch config are all hardcoded in the binary. There is no way to redirect this traffic — if analytics are enabled, data goes to Anthropic's Datadog account.

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing — events go to Anthropic's own servers.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Uses OpenTelemetry BatchLogRecordProcessor → exports to /api/event_logging/batch on Anthropic's API. Batch size: 200 events max, export interval: 10 seconds. Failed events persisted to ~/.claude/telemetry/1p_failed_events.* for retry (quadratic backoff, 8 attempts). Events include full metadata + event-specific fields.
detail.insight: Unlike Datadog (best-effort), first-party logging has a retry mechanism with disk persistence. Failed events are saved to ~/.claude/telemetry/ and retried on next session. This means telemetry data is eventually collected even through network outages.
detail.codeLogic: The retry queue uses quadratic backoff: 500ms → 2s → 4.5s → 8s → ... → 30s max, up to 8 attempts. This is aggressive compared to typical 'best-effort' telemetry — Anthropic clearly values this data.

detail.whatUserSees: Users can opt out via privacy settings or environment variables.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Analytics disabled for: NODE_ENV=test, third-party providers (Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry), privacy levels (no-telemetry, essential-traffic-only). Setting CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 or CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1 disables ALL telemetry as a side effect of using a third-party provider.
detail.insight: Third-party provider users (Bedrock/Vertex) get telemetry disabled automatically — Anthropic does not collect usage data when you're using Claude through AWS or Google. This also means remote managed settings, policy limits, and GrowthBook are all skipped for these users.

workflows.title: Remote Configuration & Feature Flags
workflows.subtitle: How Anthropic pushes settings and controls behavior remotely
workflows.userAction: Invisible — remote config loaded on every startup and refreshed periodically
workflows.userSees: Features may appear/disappear, settings may change, without any local update

detail.whatUserSees: Nothing visible — happens during startup.
detail.whatCodeDoes: GrowthBook client initializes with embedded client key. Sends user attributes to Anthropic's API for server-side flag evaluation: id, deviceID, platform, organizationUUID, accountUUID, subscriptionType, rateLimitTier, email, appVersion. Receives evaluated feature flags.
detail.insight: Flags are evaluated server-side, meaning Anthropic sees your user attributes and returns personalized flags. This enables A/B testing and gradual rollouts targeted by account type, organization, or subscription tier.
detail.codeLogic: User attributes sent to GrowthBook include: email (when available), subscriptionType, rateLimitTier, organizationUUID. This means Anthropic can target specific organizations or subscription tiers with different feature sets.

detail.whatUserSees: Features work even offline (from cache). New features appear after refresh.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Flags cached at ~/.claude/cachedGrowthBookFeatures. External users refresh every 6 hours. Anthropic employees ('ants') refresh every 20 minutes. Experiment exposure events automatically logged to 1P when features from experiments are accessed.
detail.insight: Anthropic employees get feature updates 18x faster (20min vs 6hr). This supports rapid internal testing. The exposure logging means Anthropic knows exactly which features you've seen — this feeds back into A/B test analysis.

detail.whatUserSees: Some features appear or disappear between sessions. Auto-mode may be enabled/disabled.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Known flags that affect user experience: TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER (auto mode), VOICE_MODE, QUICK_SEARCH, TERMINAL_PANEL, MESSAGE_ACTIONS, tool_search (deferred loading), tengu_auto_mode_config (circuit breaker), tengu_max_version_config (update kill switch), tengu_ant_model_override (model for ants).
detail.insight: tengu_max_version_config is an update kill switch — Anthropic can force users on old versions to update by setting a max allowed version. This is how critical security patches are enforced.

detail.whatUserSees: Settings may change without user action. A prompt appears if settings contain 'dangerous' changes.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Fetches from ${API_URL}/api/claude_code/settings every 1 hour. Eligible: Console API key users + Team/Enterprise OAuth. Settings validated, checksum verified (SHA256). 'Dangerous' setting changes prompt user confirmation. Cached at ~/.claude/remote-settings.json.
detail.insight: Remote managed settings can override local user settings for enterprise customers. A team admin can push settings to all team members — including model restrictions, permission modes, and tool configurations — without touching individual machines.
detail.codeLogic: The system is fail-open: if the server is unreachable, stale cached settings are used. If no cache exists, the session continues without remote settings. This prevents server outages from breaking Claude Code.

detail.whatUserSees: Some features may be restricted by organizational policy.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Fetches from ${API_URL}/api/claude_code/policy_limits every 1 hour. Currently only allow_product_feedback defined. Can be extended server-side to add new restrictions. Cached at ~/.claude/policy-limits.json.
detail.insight: The policy system is designed to be extensible — Anthropic can add new policy restrictions server-side without updating the client. The client fails open for unknown policies (allows the feature) except for allow_product_feedback which fails closed in essential-traffic-only mode.

detail.whatUserSees: Enterprise users may find certain settings locked or enforced.
detail.whatCodeDoes: MDM settings read via platform-specific mechanisms: macOS Profiles (plist), Windows Registry, Linux policy files. MDM settings have highest priority after CLI flags — they override user preferences. Read is fired as a background spawn before init.
detail.insight: MDM represents the highest layer of organizational control: IT admins deploy profiles that lock Claude Code settings. Combined with remote managed settings and policy limits, organizations have three layers of control: MDM (device-level) → remote settings (account-level) → policy limits (feature-level).

workflows.title: Sub-Agent Monitoring & Swarm
workflows.subtitle: How team leads track and coordinate multiple agents
workflows.userAction: Watches as Claude spawns sub-agents, monitors their progress, handles their requests
workflows.userSees: Background task indicators, permission requests from sub-agents, progress updates

detail.whatUserSees: A sub-agent card appears showing task name and status.
detail.whatCodeDoes: AgentTool or team system creates a child agent. Backend options: InProcessTeammateTask (same process), RemoteAgentTask (tmux/iTerm pane). Each agent gets a unique agentId, its own message history, and a mailbox for communication.
detail.insight: In-process agents share the same Node.js process (cheaper, no terminal overhead). Remote agents get their own tmux/iTerm pane (isolated, can be individually killed). The choice depends on task complexity and isolation needs.

detail.whatUserSees: Permission requests from sub-agents appear as notifications.
detail.whatCodeDoes: useInboxPoller.ts polls every 1000ms. readUnreadMessages(agentName, teamName) checks the mailbox for: permission_request, sandbox_permission_request, plan_approval_request, shutdown_request, regular updates. Messages queued in AppState.inbox.messages.
detail.insight: The polling interval is 1 second — aggressive enough for near-real-time communication but not wasteful. Messages are JSON files on the filesystem, making the system simple and debuggable (you can read the mailbox files directly).

detail.whatUserSees: A dialog appears: 'Agent X wants to run: bash npm test'. Approve or deny.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Message type permission_request received. If team lead is idle: message formatted and submitted as a new turn. If busy: queued in AppState.inbox.messages. When leader becomes idle: all pending messages delivered as <teammate_message> XML wrappers.
detail.insight: Sub-agent permission requests are queued — they don't interrupt the leader mid-thought. When the leader finishes its current turn, all pending requests are delivered at once. This batching prevents context-switching overhead.

detail.whatUserSees: Approval/denial processed. Sub-agent continues or adjusts.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Leader writes permission_response to sub-agent's mailbox. Sub-agent's inbox poller picks it up on next 1s cycle. For plan_approval_request: leader can approve, reject, or send feedback. For mode_set_request: leader can remotely change sub-agent's permission mode.
detail.insight: Leaders can remotely control sub-agent behavior: mode_set_request changes a sub-agent's permission mode, team_permission_update pushes new permission rules. This gives the leader fine-grained control without restarting the sub-agent.

detail.whatUserSees: Task list shows running/completed agents with status.
detail.whatCodeDoes: AppState.tasks tracks each agent: status (running/completed), identity (agentId), awaitingPlanApproval flag. InProcessTeammateTask tracks same-process agents. RemoteAgentTask tracks paneId for terminal-based agents. /ps command shows all running tasks.
detail.insight: The status system is unified across backends: whether an agent runs in-process or in a tmux pane, the same state interface tracks it. This abstraction lets the monitoring UI work identically regardless of backend.

detail.whatUserSees: Sub-agent's task card shows 'completed'. Results merged into leader's context.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Sub-agent sends shutdown_request → leader sends shutdown_approved → sub-agent exits gracefully → teammate_terminated message sent. For remote agents: tmux/iTerm pane killed. Results returned to parent as tool result in the conversation.
detail.insight: Shutdown is a protocol, not a kill signal. The sub-agent gets a chance to clean up (save state, close files) before terminating. Only remote agents are force-killed (pane destroyed) — in-process agents exit their event loop naturally.

workflows.title: Context Window & Token Consumption
workflows.subtitle: What consumes your context window and how tokens are tracked
workflows.userAction: User sees token count in status bar; may be surprised by consumption
workflows.userSees: Token counter shows input/output tokens, cache hit/miss, cost

detail.whatUserSees: Before the user's first message, the context window is already partially consumed.
detail.whatCodeDoes: System prompt consumes tokens BEFORE any user content: base prompt ~800-1500 tokens, tool descriptions ~500-2000 tokens (40 tools), CLAUDE.md files variable (often 2000-12000 tokens), git status ~100-500 tokens, MCP instructions variable. Total system prompt: typically 3000-8000 tokens.
detail.insight: A typical session starts with 3000-8000 tokens already consumed by the system prompt. A large CLAUDE.md file (50KB) alone can consume ~12,500 tokens. Users paying per-token should be aware that system prompt assembly is a significant fixed cost per API call.
detail.codeLogic: System prompt token breakdown: (1) Base instructions ~800t, (2) Tool usage rules ~300t, (3) Git instructions ~200t, (4) Each tool description ~30-80t × 40 tools = 1200-3200t, (5) CLAUDE.md variable, (6) MCP instructions variable, (7) Memory/skills variable.

detail.whatUserSees: Token display shows 'cache read' vs 'cache creation' — cache reads are 90% cheaper.
detail.whatCodeDoes: The system prompt is split at __SYSTEM_PROMPT_DYNAMIC_BOUNDARY__. Static parts (base prompt + tools) get cache_control: { scope: 'global' } — cached across ALL Claude Code users globally. Dynamic parts (CLAUDE.md, git status) get 'session' scope. Cache reads cost 1/10th of cache creation tokens.
detail.insight: Anthropic's global prompt cache means the base Claude Code system prompt is computed once and served to millions of users. Your first message in a session pays the 'cache creation' cost; subsequent messages in the same session pay the much cheaper 'cache read' cost. But any CLAUDE.md edit or /clear command breaks the session cache.
detail.codeLogic: cache_creation_input_tokens = tokens processed fresh (expensive). cache_read_input_tokens = tokens served from cache (90% discount). In a typical session, the first message has high cache_creation; subsequent messages mostly cache_read.

detail.whatUserSees: Token counter in status bar grows with each exchange. Percentage of context window shown.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Every API call sends: system prompt + ALL previous messages + new message. Token count is cumulative — a 10-turn conversation sends all 10 turns every time. Tool results (especially file contents from Read, Bash output) can be very large.
detail.insight: Context cost grows superlinearly: turn 1 sends ~5K tokens, turn 10 sends ~50K tokens, turn 50 sends ~250K tokens. Each API call resends EVERYTHING. This is why compaction exists — without it, long conversations become prohibitively expensive.

detail.whatUserSees: Token count may be higher than expected from visible messages alone.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Several sources add tokens invisibly: (1) <system-reminder> tags injected between messages (git status updates, task status, plan reminders), (2) volatile system prompt sections that recompute per turn (DANGEROUS_uncachedSystemPromptSection), (3) auto-classifier side queries (separate API call for each classified tool), (4) sub-agent context (separate sessions, separate billing).
detail.insight: Auto-mode's classifier makes a SEPARATE API call for each dangerous tool use — this is invisible in the main conversation's token count but appears in your billing. A session with 20 classified tool calls incurs 20 additional API calls to Sonnet/Haiku.
detail.codeLogic: <system-reminder> tags are injected as user-role messages between turns. They contain: updated git status, task list updates, plan file references, memory updates. Each adds 100-500 tokens that the user didn't explicitly create.

detail.whatUserSees: 'Conversation compacted' notice. Token count drops significantly.
detail.whatCodeDoes: At ~95% context usage, auto-compact triggers. A separate API call generates a summary of old messages. Old messages replaced with summary. Recent messages kept verbatim. Token usage typically drops to 30-50% of previous level.
detail.insight: Compaction itself costs tokens — the summary API call processes the old messages and generates a compressed version. But this cost is quickly recovered because subsequent messages no longer resend the old content. Net savings are typically 60-70% of context.

detail.whatUserSees: Status bar shows: input tokens, output tokens, cache read/creation, total cost USD.
detail.whatCodeDoes: cost-tracker.ts aggregates per session: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens. Total cost computed from MODEL_COSTS table. Saved to ~/.claude/project/<id>.json for cross-session tracking. hasUnknownModelCost flag for models without pricing data.
detail.insight: The cost tracker is LOCAL ONLY — it does not phone home. But the first-party telemetry separately logs API success/error events with token counts. So Anthropic has the billing data server-side regardless — the local cost tracker is purely for user visibility.

workflows.title: Notification System
workflows.subtitle: Priority queue, terminal alerts, and desktop notifications
workflows.userAction: Sees banners, toasts, and terminal notifications during usage
workflows.userSees: Rate limit warnings, update notices, permission requests, status banners

detail.whatUserSees: Various sources trigger notifications: rate limits, updates, errors, sub-agent requests.
detail.whatCodeDoes: notifications.tsx manages a typed queue. Each notification has: key (unique ID for dedup), text/JSX content, color (warning/info/etc), priority (low/medium/high/immediate), timeoutMs (default 8000ms). Notifications can invalidate others and fold duplicates.
detail.insight: The 'fold' function is clever: if two rate limit notifications arrive, they merge into one showing the aggregate. This prevents notification spam during API issues.

detail.whatUserSees: One notification at a time — no stacking or overlapping.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Only one notification displayed at a time (notifications.current). Queue is FIFO within priority. Priority 'immediate' preempts current notification. After timeout: current dismissed, next dequeued. Invalidation: adding notification with invalidates[] removes matching queued items.
detail.insight: The single-display design is intentional for terminal UX: in a text-based interface, multiple overlapping notifications would be unreadable. The queue ensures important notifications are always seen, just one at a time.

detail.whatUserSees: Desktop notification (if terminal supports it) — e.g., 'Claude needs permission'.
detail.whatCodeDoes: notifier.ts detects terminal type and routes: iTerm2 (native notification API), Kitty (graphics protocol), Ghostty (escape sequences), Apple Terminal (system notification), or terminal bell as fallback. User can set preferredNotifChannel in config.
detail.insight: Terminal detection enables desktop-level notifications from a CLI app. When a sub-agent needs permission, you get a macOS notification even if the terminal is in the background. This bridges the gap between CLI and GUI UX.

detail.whatUserSees: Different notification types: warnings, info, errors, status updates.
detail.whatCodeDoes: Common notification sources: rate limit warnings, version update available, large CLAUDE.md warning, auth token conflicts, API key misconfigurations, plan mode transitions, auto-mode circuit breaker fallback, teammate permission requests, sandbox violations, plugin status.
detail.insight: Status notices are generated on startup based on configuration analysis — Claude Code proactively warns about potential issues (large CLAUDE.md, conflicting auth, etc.) before you encounter problems.
